comments:
This paper presents an interesting interdisciplinary reflection and practice at the intersection of computer science (notions of intension vs extension, data-mining/interactive search) and contemporary theatre practice. The authors give a clear conceptual contrast between a traditional ontology-driven approach to similarity (instantiation, dramaturgical types) and an interactive, extension-oriented approach inspired by data-mining and music-ripping practices. The paper is strengthened by a concrete implementation and artistic case study: the inter-media performance La traversée de la nuit, using a supervised neural network to classify vocal emotional states and a multi-agent system to generate projected images in real time.

Strengths:
- Original and timely interdisciplinary framing linking AI concepts to theatrical practice.
- A concrete implemented system is described (voice feature vector design, supervised training, multi-agent image generator), showing artistic validity and practical experimentation.
- The discussion captures interesting conceptual consequences: shifting from predefined ontologies to emergent, interactive creation and the heuristic role of the machine.

Weaknesses and points needing improvement:
- Technical details are sparse. Important implementation specifics are missing: neural network architecture, size of training data, training/validation methodology, recognition accuracy, latency and real-time constraints, and parameterization of the multi-agent system (agent counts, utility functions, time scales). These details are necessary for assessing technical soundness and reproducibility.
- No systematic evaluation. The paper lacks objective or structured assessment of the system’s behaviour (e.g., recognition performance), or of the artistic outcome (audience reaction, performer experience, robustness in live conditions). Qualitative comments from performers or audience would strengthen the claims about emergent dramaturgy.
- Related work coverage is limited. The manuscript would benefit from situating the work within literature on affective computing / emotion recognition, interactive stage systems, and prior agent-based generative visuals to highlight novelty and differences.
- The theoretical distinction between intension and extension is stimulating but remains at a high level; formalization or more detailed examples showing how the system departs from an ontology-driven design would help readers from either discipline.
- Presentation sometimes repeats the same ideas; tighter organization and clearer figures or screenshots (or pointers to video) would improve clarity.

Recommendations to authors:
- Add technical appendices describing the neural net architecture, feature extraction pipeline, dataset size, training procedure, and empirical performance metrics (confusion matrix, latency measurements).
- Include qualitative evaluation: structured feedback from the performers, rehearsal notes, or audience comments; a short usability/robustness report from live shows.
- Expand the related-work section to acknowledge and contrast relevant work in affect recognition and interactive performance systems.
- If possible, provide visual examples (stills or links to video) and diagrams of the multi-agent system and its communication/utility model.

Overall assessment: The paper is a valuable contribution to digital arts and human-machine performance practice, offering a clear conceptual lens and an implemented system. However, for a computer science audience it currently lacks the technical and evaluation detail needed to fully assess the claims. I recommend acceptance in a venue appropriate for demonstrations/poster presentations after revision to include more technical and evaluative information.
